title: What is a map
instruction: A map lets you look up a key (like an ISBN) and get its value (like a book title). Run this to look up two books by ISBN.
successMessage: Look up by ISBN, get the book title. That's the entire concept of a map.

title: Declare with make
instruction: `make(map[string]int)` creates an empty map. Add two ISBNs with copy counts (3 and 5) and print the map.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: make(map[keyType]valueType) — empty catalog, ready to fill.

title: Add and update
instruction: Adding and updating use the same syntax: `copies["ISBN"] = N`. Add a new book with 6 copies, then update an existing one to 7, then print.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Same syntax for insert and update. If the ISBN exists, the count changes. If not, it's created.

title: Read a value and check if it exists 🤯
instruction: Reading a missing key returns zero — silently. But `count, ok := copies["ISBN"]` tells you if it truly exists (ok=true) or not (ok=false). Check an ISBN that exists and one that doesn't.
hint: count, ok := copies["978-0-14-044913-6"] then fmt.Println(count, ok). Repeat for a missing ISBN.

codeChecks.message: Use the two-value form: count, ok := copies["978-0-14-044913-6"]
Also check for a missing ISBN.
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: count, ok — the ok idiom. It's Go's standard pattern for safe lookups. You'll use this everywhere.

title: Delete a key
instruction: `delete(map, key)` removes a key-value pair. Delete two books from the catalog and print the result.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your delete calls.

successMessage: delete() takes the map and the key. Two books removed, the rest unaffected.

title: Loop over a map
instruction: `for isbn, count := range catalog` iterates every entry. Important: maps are unordered — the order may change each run. Loop and print each ISBN and its count.
hint: for isbn, count := range catalog { fmt.Printf("%s: %d\n", isbn, count) }

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for isbn, count := range catalog
Use fmt.Printf to format the output.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Looping works. But note: the output order may vary each run — maps have no guaranteed order.

title: Map with slice values
instruction: A map's value can be a slice: `map[string][]string` maps a member ID to their checked-out books. Add member M003 with two books, then print all.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: map[string][]string — one member ID, many borrowed books. You'll see this pattern a lot.

title: Build a genre counter
instruction: Loop through a genres slice and count each genre. `genreCounts[genre]++` works because Go returns 0 for missing keys. Add the loop and print the counts.
hint: for _, genre := range genres { genreCounts[genre]++ } then fmt.Println(genreCounts)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Genre counter done. This exact pattern powers library analytics and circulation reports.
